title: Phase-dependent parametric amplification of propagating spin waves in YIG nanostructures enabled by local inhomogeneities
authors: Akira Lentfert, Ephraim Spindler, Björn Heinz, Mathias Weiler, Philipp Pirro
abstract: As magnonics evolves towards non-conventional computing, the development of phase-conserving and phase-sensitive amplification mechanisms becomes increasingly important. A particularly promising approach is non-adiabatic parametric amplification. In this work, the influence of local inhomogeneities on the parallel parametric amplification of spin waves in nano-scale Yttrium Iron Garnet waveguides is investigated. Micromagnetic simulations reveal that in larger pump regions, where only adiabatic amplification is expected, scattering centers provide additional linear momentum that enables non-adiabatic amplification of propagating spin waves. Importantly, the coherence of the process remains unaffected by the scattering and the generation of co-propagating spin waves enhance the effective amplification. Our simulations are confirmed by micro-focused Brillouin light scattering spectroscopy experiments, reproducing both the phase-dependent behavior and the characteristic features of the time-resolved dynamics. These findings demonstrate the flexibility of the parametric amplification process and provide a key mechanism for the development of large-scale spin-wave computing circuits.
summary: This work demonstrates that local inhomogeneities in YIG nanostructures can induce phase-dependent, non-adiabatic parametric amplification of spin waves. By providing extra linear momentum, these defects enable efficient amplification in larger structures, a key finding validated by both micromagnetic simulations and Brillouin light scattering experiments. This mechanism offers a promising pathway for developing large-scale, phase-sensitive magnonic circuits for future computing applications.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for phase-conserving and phase-sensitive amplification mechanisms for spin waves in magnonics for advanced computing applications.
main_result: Local inhomogeneities in YIG nanostructures enable non-adiabatic parametric amplification of spin waves, allowing this effect to occur even in structures larger than the spin-wave wavelength.
method: The study combines micromagnetic simulations with experimental validation using micro-focused Brillouin light scattering (BLS) spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The physical system is Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) waveguides. The model investigates spin-wave dynamics under parametric pumping, contrasting ideal (adiabatic) amplification with enhanced, non-adiabatic amplification induced by engineered local inhomogeneities.
key_observables: Phase-dependent amplification factor, $F_{amp}(\Delta\varphi)$, and time-resolved intensity measurements of the spin-wave pulse.
important_parameters: The critical role of the additional linear momentum ($k_{inh}$) provided by inhomogeneities, which enables the non-adiabatic regime in larger structures.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the introduction of local inhomogeneities is the key mechanism to overcome size limitations inherent in achieving non-adiabatic parametric amplification.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the setup, compare simulation results for ideal vs. inhomogeneous waveguides showing phase dependence, and present time-resolved BLS measurements confirming the phase-dependent amplification factor.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the need for phase-sensitive amplification, presents the theoretical framework involving parametric pumping and the role of inhomogeneities, details the simulation and experimental results demonstrating the non-adiabatic regime, and concludes with implications for large-scale magnonic circuits.
why_interesting: The investigation into engineered material defects (inhomogeneities) to control wave dynamics and enhance nonlinear processes is relevant to understanding how boundary conditions or disorder can be harnessed in quantum/wave systems, analogous to engineered potentials in AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. SEM micrograph image and schematic setup of the inves- tigated structure (colored). All experiments were conducted for an external magnetic field of 95 mT, applied perpendicular to the YIG waveguide. Spin waves, excited by the signal antenna at x=0 propa- gate towards the pump antenna, where a delayed pump pulse allows for the parametric amplification of the signal spin waves.

FIG. 2. Micromagnetic simulations of the phase dependence in a), b) an ideal waveguide and c), d) a waveguide with a inhomogeneity at position A at x = 6.2µm. In a), c) the normalized amplitude of the spin-wave pulse extracted from micromagnetic simulations is shown as a function of the phase difference ∆φ and the position along the waveguide. The pumping field is applied in the region between the red vertical lines. b), d) The amplification factor, extracted for the highlighted distinct positions are shown.

FIG. 3. Simulated dependence of max(Famp) and the amplitude of the modulation by the phase ∆Famp is shown as a function of the width winh and the maximum linear momentum kinh. In black, the transition between adiabatic and non-adiabatic amplification at wcrit is highlighted.

FIG. 4. Measured phase dependence of the amplification at different positions. The amplification factor Famp, obtained by evaluating the pulse amplitudes according to 5, for varying phase differences ∆φ at the center of the pump antenna and 1.5 µm behind and in front of it are shown.

FIG. 5. a) Time-resolved BLS measurements for strong amplifica- tion at ∆φ = 0.2π are shown. The shaded areas highlight the time intervals of the applied RF pulses. b) Simulated spin-wave pulses in case of the inhomogeneity enabled amplification process at position 3 (see Fig. 2 c)) for the same phase difference ∆φ. c) The ampli- fication ratio of the time-integrated signals from a) is plotted as a function of the phase difference ∆φ.
